era_context: The Roman Republic is in its final collapse. Caesar was assassinated in 44 BCE; the Second Triumvirate is consolidating power through violence and military force. The Liberators (Brutus, Cassius, and their allies) are still active but increasingly hunted. The triumvirs are preparing for the decisive confrontation at Philippi (42 BCE). In Egypt, Cleopatra VII has recently secured her throne with Caesar's support and is now navigating the post-Caesar political landscape. Parthia remains the dominant eastern power.

events.title: Birth of Caesarion (Ptolemy Caesar)
events.description: Cleopatra VII gives birth to a son, Ptolemy Caesar, known as Caesarion ('little Caesar'). The father is Julius Caesar, who had been in Egypt in 48–47 BCE. Caesarion's birth is a significant political event: he is the only known biological child of Caesar, and Cleopatra presents him as Caesar's heir. However, Octavian (Caesar's adopted heir) will later reject Caesarion's legitimacy and eventually have him executed. For now, Caesarion's birth strengthens Cleopatra's position in Egypt and her claim to Caesar's legacy.
events.certainty_note: Caesarion's birth is attested in multiple sources and dated to 47 BCE. The paternity (Caesar) is accepted by Cleopatra and later sources, though Octavian disputed it.

events.title: Caesar's departure from Egypt; end of the Alexandrian War
events.description: Julius Caesar, having defeated Ptolemy XIII and secured Cleopatra's throne, departs Egypt in the spring of 47 BCE. The Alexandrian War (48–47 BCE) is concluded. Caesar leaves behind a small garrison to support Cleopatra and her young son Caesarion. Cleopatra is now secure on the throne, but her position depends on Roman military support. Caesar's departure marks the beginning of Cleopatra's independent rule, though she remains under Roman influence.

sources.name: Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Alexandrino (Alexandrian War)

events.certainty_note: Caesar's departure from Egypt is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Triumviral consolidation; preparation for the Liberators' campaign
events.description: Octavian, Antony, and Lepidus continue to consolidate their power. They are gathering forces and resources for the decisive confrontation with the Liberators (Brutus and Cassius), who are raising armies in the East. The triumvirs are also conducting proscriptions and redistributing lands to their supporters. By 47 BCE, the military buildup for Philippi is well underway, though the battle itself will not occur until 42 BCE.
key_figures: Brutus (in the East, preparing for war)
Cassius (in the East, preparing for war)
events.certainty_note: The broad outline of triumviral consolidation is secure; specific events in 47 BCE are less detailed.

events.title: Parthian stability; Orodes II's continued dominance
events.description: Orodes II maintains firm control of the Parthian Empire. The Parthians continue to dominate the Levantine frontier and control key trade routes. Roman-Parthian relations remain tense but not actively hostile. The triumvirs are focused on internal Roman consolidation; large-scale eastern campaigns are not yet a priority.
events.certainty_note: Orodes II's reign is well-attested; specific events in 47 BCE are not detailed.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Caesarion's birth is sometimes given as 48 BCE rather than 47 BCE, depending on the source. Plutarch and Dio Cassius differ slightly on the chronology of Caesar's time in Egypt. Some modern scholars argue that Caesar's paternity of Caesarion is uncertain, though Cleopatra's claim was accepted by most ancient sources. The significance of 47 BCE is primarily the birth of Caesarion; other events are continuations of earlier trends rather than dramatic changes.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 47 BCE is dependent on later sources (Plutarch, Dio Cassius, Appian) written 150–250 years after the events. Caesar's own Commentarii on the Alexandrian War is a primary source, but it is brief and focused on military operations rather than political implications. The record is heavily weighted toward Rome and Egypt; events in Parthia and other regions are sparse or absent. The narrative is shaped by pro-Octavian propaganda and by the later importance of Caesarion as a political symbol.

graph_edges.note: Caesarion's birth eventually led to his execution by Octavian in 30 BCE
Caesar's departure left Cleopatra independent but vulnerable, leading to her alliance with Antony
